perturbation.original_text: Contractor is performing the Services as an independent contractor and not as an employee of Company and none of Contractor's personnel shall be entitled to receive any compensation, benefits or other incidents of employment from Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor is performing the Services as an independent contractor and not as an employee of Company. However, Contractor's personnel shall be entitled to receive benefits such as health insurance and paid time off, as per standard employment practices in Arizona.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates a contradiction in the classification of the Contractor. While stating that the Contractor is an independent entity, it then mandates that Contractor's personnel receive benefits typically reserved for employees, creating ambiguity and potential misclassification under labor laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-601 - Definition of Employment
perturbation.law_explanation: ARS § 23-601 defines 'employment' in Arizona. By entitling the 'independent contractor' personnel to employee benefits, the contract language implies an employer-employee relationship, directly contradicting the definition and potentially triggering employer obligations.

The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-601 - Declaration of policy 23-601 . Declaration of policy As a guide to the interpretation and application of this chapter, the public policy of this state is declared to be as follows: Economic insecurity due to unemployment is a serious menace to the health, morals and welfare of the people of this state. Involuntary unemployment is therefore a subject of general interest and concern which requires appropriate action by the legislature to prevent its spread and to lighten its burden which now so often falls with crushing force upon the unemployed worker and his family. The achievement of social security requires protection against this greatest hazard of economic life. This can be provided by encouraging employers to provide more stable employment and by the systematic accumulation of funds during periods of employment to provide benefits for periods of unemployment, thus maintaining purchasing power and limiting the serious social consequences of poor relief assistance. The legislature, therefore, declares that in its considered judgment the public good and the general welfare of the citizens of this state require the enactment of this measure, under the police powers of the state, for the compulsory setting aside of unemployment reserves to be used for the benefit of persons unemployed through no fault of their own. perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction by stating the contractor is an independent contractor but then entitling the contractor's personnel to employee benefits. The Law Explanation references ARS § 23-601, defining 'employment' in Arizona, and indicates that providing employee benefits implies an employer-employee relationship, which contradicts the independent contractor status initially stated in the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which includes the policy declaration in 23-601, reinforces this by emphasizing the importance of distinguishing employment and unemployment for the purpose of social security and unemployment benefits. The changed text's provision of benefits typically associated with employment directly undermines the intended legal distinction between independent contractors and employees, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Arizona, without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Arizona, specifically including its conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Conflict of laws provisions determine which jurisdiction's laws apply when there are conflicting laws from different jurisdictions. By explicitly including conflict of laws provisions, the application of Arizona law becomes uncertain as the court may be required to consider the laws of another jurisdiction. This could significantly alter the interpretation and enforcement of the contract, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws provides principles for determining which jurisdiction's law applies when multiple jurisdictions have an interest in the subject matter. This modified term now indicates conflict of laws provision which makes it less clear that Arizona law will govern, potentially applying laws from other jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Arizona law governs the agreement. The changed text introduces the 'conflict of laws provisions,' which, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, deals with 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' This directly contradicts the intent of applying Arizona law exclusively, as it opens the door for other jurisdictions' laws to be considered, thus undermining the original governing law provision.

perturbation.original_text: Throughout the term of this Agreement, Contractor shall maintain workers compensation insurance in the amount required by statute, comprehensive general liability insurance with coverage of at least one million dollars ($1,000,000) and professional errors and omissions insurance for bodily injury, property damage or other losses with coverage of at least one million dollars ($1,000,000), in connection with the provision of Services by Contractor pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Throughout the term of this Agreement, Contractor may maintain insurance coverage at their discretion. However, if damages arise from the contractor's omissions, the contractor will be responsible for one million dollars ($1,000,000).
perturbation.explanation: This removes the obligatory language that the Contractor *shall* maintain insurance and makes it discretionary. It eliminates workers compensation and general liability, violating standard business practice and significantly altering risk allocation. Furthermore, it contradicts the definition of insurance by only stating the contractor will be responsible for damages should an omission occur, rather than maintain insurance to cover potential omissions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-902. Employer's duty to secure compensation
perturbation.law_explanation: A.R.S. § 23-902 mandates that employers in Arizona secure workers' compensation insurance for their employees. By removing the requirement for worker's compensation insurance and insurance generally, this changed text contradicts the employer's duty under Arizona law if the 'independent contractor' is actually considered an employee, and opens the contractor to significant liability.

DISCLAIMER This online version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is primarily maintained for legislative drafting purposes and reflects the version of law that is effective on January 1st of the year following the most recent legislative session. The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-902 - Employers subject to chapter; exceptions 23-902. Employers subject to chapter; exceptions A. Employers subject to this chapter are the state, each county, city, town, municipal corporation and school district and every person who employs any workers or operatives regularly employed in the same business or establishment under contract of hire, including covered employees pursuant to a professional employer agreement, except domestic servants. Exempted employers of domestic servants may come under this chapter by complying with its provisions and the rules of the commission. For the purposes of this subsection, "regularly employed" includes all employments, whether continuous throughout the year, or for only a portion of the year, in the usual trade, business, profession or occupation of an employer. B. When an employer procures work to be done for the employer by a contractor over whose work the employer retains supervision or control, and the work is a part or process in the trade or business of the employer, then the contractors and the contractor's employees, and any subcontractor and the subcontractor's employees, are, within the meaning of this section, employees of the original employer. For the purposes of this subsection, "part or process in the trade or business of the employer" means a particular work activity that in the context of an ongoing and integral business process is regular, ordinary or routine in the operation of the business or is routinely done through the business' own employees. C. A person engaged in work for a business, and who while so engaged is independent of that business in the execution of the work and not subject to the rule or control of the business for which the work is done, but is engaged only in the performance of a definite job or piece of work, and is subordinate to that business only in effecting a result in accordance with that business design, is an independent contractor. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit requirement for the Contractor to maintain worker's compensation insurance and general liability insurance, making insurance coverage discretionary. This directly contradicts A.R.S. § 23-902, which mandates that employers secure workers' compensation insurance for their employees, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing A.R.S. 23-902, states that 'Employers subject to this chapter are the state, each county, city, town, municipal corporation and school district and every person who employs any workers or operatives regularly employed in the same business or establishment under contract of hire...'. This establishes a clear legal obligation that the modified text undermines.
